query: prompt library architecture for vibe coders and agentic builders

findings.title: Why prompt libraries matter for AI-native product teams
findings.excerpt: Builders who maintain organized prompt libraries report 40–60% reduction in repeated context setup per session. The key insight: prompts are executable assets, not chat history.
findings.relevanceNote: Directly relevant to Struq's core value proposition

findings.title: Taxonomy of reusable AI instructions
findings.excerpt: Three categories emerge consistently: (1) context primes — one-time setup prompts, (2) task recipes — repeatable instruction templates, (3) constraint sets — rules about what NOT to do. Most builders conflate these.
findings.relevanceNote: Maps to Struq's asset taxonomy (prompts, skills, context)

findings.title: Skill packs as composable instruction modules
findings.excerpt: The most effective prompt libraries organize instructions into domain-specific skill packs. A 'testing skill' knows how your project writes tests. An 'API route skill' knows your auth patterns. These compose at generation time.
findings.relevanceNote: Validates the skill-pack approach in Struq Learn

findings.title: Agentic building patterns for solo developers
findings.excerpt: Solo builders benefit most from instruction systems that reduce 'cold start' friction — the cost of re-explaining your project to a new AI session. Context files and skill packs are the primary tools for this.

synthesized: Research confirms that organized prompt and skill libraries are a high-leverage practice for AI-native builders. The core insight: prompts should be treated as executable assets, not temporary chat messages. The most effective libraries use a three-tier structure: context primes (project setup), task recipes (repeatable instruction templates), and constraint sets (what NOT to do). Domain-specific skill packs that compose at generation time are the advanced form of this. For solo vibe coders, reducing cold-start friction between sessions is the primary value driver.
